quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Compound microscope"?

options.text: Two converging lenses (objective + eyepiece); total magnification M = m_objective × M_eyepiece; enlarges nearby small objects
Uses r = mv/(qB) to separate ions by mass: heavier ions curve less (larger r), lighter ions curve more (smaller r)
B = μ₀I/(2πr); circular field lines around a long straight wire; strength decreases with distance r from wire
ΣΔV = 0 around any closed loop; based on conservation of energy; EMF gains = resistive drops

quiz_questions.explanation: "Compound microscope" is defined as: Two converging lenses (objective + eyepiece); total magnification M = m_objective × M_eyepiece; enlarges nearby small objects. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Refracting telescope"?

options.text: The total entropy of an isolated system never decreases; natural processes are irreversible
d_o > 0 (real object); d_i > 0 (real image, opposite side); d_i  0 (converging); f < 0 (diverging)
Objective lens collects light from distant objects; eyepiece magnifies; M = f_objective / f_eyepiece; image is inverted
Many slits produce very sharp bright fringes at d sin θ = mλ; used for precise wavelength measurement (spectroscopy)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Refracting telescope" is defined as: Objective lens collects light from distant objects; eyepiece magnifies; M = f_objective / f_eyepiece; image is inverted. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Reflecting telescope"?

options.text: Ground state: lowest energy (n=1); excited states: n > 1; atoms absorb energy to reach excited states, emit to return
ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2; fundamental limit on simultaneous measurement precision; not due to instrument limitations but inherent to nature
Uses a concave mirror as objective; avoids chromatic aberration; Newtonian, Cassegrain, and Schmidt-Cassegrain designs
The minimum energy needed to eject an electron from a material's surface; material-dependent; determines threshold frequency f₀ = φ/h

quiz_questions.explanation: "Reflecting telescope" is defined as: Uses a concave mirror as objective; avoids chromatic aberration; Newtonian, Cassegrain, and Schmidt-Cassegrain designs. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Chromatic aberration"?

options.text: Point fingers in direction of v (or I), curl toward B; thumb points in force direction for positive charge
η_Carnot = 1 - T_C/T_H; the maximum possible efficiency for an engine operating between temperatures T_H and T_C (in Kelvin)
F = kq₁q₂/r²; k = 8.99 × 10⁹ N·m²/C²; inversely proportional to r²; analogous to gravitational force
Different wavelengths focus at different points (dispersion in lenses); corrected by achromatic doublets (crown + flint glass)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Chromatic aberration" is defined as: Different wavelengths focus at different points (dispersion in lenses); corrected by achromatic doublets (crown + flint glass). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Angular magnification"?

options.text: Energy needed to completely remove an electron: for hydrogen, E_ionization = 13.6 eV (from n=1 to n=∞)
k = R/N_A = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K; relates temperature to individual molecular energy
M = θ_image / θ_object; how much larger objects appear through the instrument compared to the naked eye
Node at closed end, antinode at open: f_n = nv/(4L), n = 1,3,5,... (odd harmonics only); e.g., clarinet, stopped pipe

quiz_questions.explanation: "Angular magnification" is defined as: M = θ_image / θ_object; how much larger objects appear through the instrument compared to the naked eye. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Two converging lenses (objective + eyepiece); total magnification M = m_objective × M_eyepiece; enlarges nearby small objects"?

options.text: Magnetic force on a charge
Right-hand rule (force)
Compound microscope
Malus's law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Compound microscope".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Objective lens collects light from distant objects; eyepiece magnifies; M = f_objective / f_eyepiece; image is inverted"?

options.text: Nuclear binding energy
Standing waves
Refracting telescope
Nuclear medicine

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Refracting telescope".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Uses a concave mirror as objective; avoids chromatic aberration; Newtonian, Cassegrain, and Schmidt-Cassegrain designs"?

options.text: Wave interference
Doppler effect
Chain reaction
Reflecting telescope

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Reflecting telescope".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Different wavelengths focus at different points (dispersion in lenses); corrected by achromatic doublets (crown + flint glass)"?

options.text: Chromatic aberration
Self-inductance
Brewster's angle
Magnetic flux

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Chromatic aberration".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "M = θ_image / θ_object; how much larger objects appear through the instrument compared to the naked eye"?

options.text: Snell's law
Angular magnification
Sound level addition
Electric potential (voltage)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Angular magnification".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Optical Instruments is accurate?

options.text: An absorption spectrum shows dark lines at the same wavelengths when continuous light passes through a cool gas—the gas absorbs photons matching its transition energies.
A parallel-plate capacitor has capacitance C = ε₀A/d (area A, plate separation d).
Optical Instruments combine lenses and mirrors to extend human vision.
Curie temperature: above this temperature, ferromagnets lose their permanent magnetism and become paramagnetic.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Optical Instruments as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Optical Instruments?

options.text: An absorption spectrum shows dark lines at the same wavelengths when continuous light passes through a cool gas—the gas absorbs photons matching its transition energies.
Compound microscopes use two converging lenses: the objective (short f) creates a magnified real image, and the eyepiece (longer f) further magnifies this as a virtual image.
Brewster's angle: reflected light is completely polarized when tan θ_B = n₂/n₁.
Young's double-slit experiment demonstrates wave nature: constructive interference occurs at d sin θ = mλ (bright fringes, m = 0,±1,±2,...); destructive at d sin θ = (m+½)λ (dark fringes).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Optical Instruments as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Optical Instruments?

options.text: Telescopes use an objective lens/mirror to collect light from distant objects and an eyepiece to magnify the image.
Ideal Gas Behavior is described by the ideal gas law: PV = nRT (or PV = NkT), relating pressure P, volume V, temperature T, and amount of gas n (moles) or N (molecules).
The Heisenberg uncertainty principle: ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2—the position and momentum of a particle cannot both be precisely known simultaneously.
Curie temperature: above this temperature, ferromagnets lose their permanent magnetism and become paramagnetic.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Optical Instruments as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Optical Instruments?

options.text: Total magnification = M_objective × M_eyepiece.
Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave produced by vibrating objects.
Key properties: wavelength (λ), frequency (f), amplitude (A), and wave speed (v = fλ).
The force on a current loop creates torque: τ = nIAB sin θ, the basis for electric motors.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Optical Instruments as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Optical Instruments?

options.text: Aberrations (chromatic, spherical) limit image quality and are corrected using compound lens systems.
The resolution limit of optical instruments is set by the Rayleigh criterion.
An emission spectrum consists of bright lines at specific wavelengths produced when excited gas atoms emit photons during electron transitions downward.
Magnetic Fields and Forces explores the interaction between magnetic fields and charged particles.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Optical Instruments as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Optical Instruments?

options.text: Energy storage: supercapacitors bridge the gap between batteries and conventional capacitors, providing rapid charge/discharge for regenerative braking in electric vehicles
Wireless charging (Qi standard): a changing magnetic field in the charging pad induces an EMF in the receiving coil inside the phone
Electron microscopes achieve resolutions of ~0.1 nm by using electron 'lenses' (magnetic fields) instead of optical lenses—visualizing individual atoms
PET scans detect positron annihilation: the 1.022 MeV of mass-energy from an electron-positron pair converts to two 0.511 MeV gamma rays traveling in opposite directions

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Optical Instruments.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Optical Instruments?

options.text: Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse) travel through Earth at different speeds, allowing scientists to locate earthquake epicenters and map Earth's interior structure
The James Webb Space Telescope uses a 6.5m segmented gold-coated mirror to collect infrared light from the earliest galaxies in the universe
Car side mirrors are convex ('objects in mirror are closer than they appear') to provide a wider field of view, reducing blind spots
Nuclear weapons: a 20-kiloton bomb converts only about 1 gram of matter to energy (E = mc² = 0.001 kg × 9 × 10¹⁶ = 9 × 10¹³ J ≈ 20 kilotons of TNT)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Optical Instruments.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Optical Instruments applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: The Sun converts 600 million tons of hydrogen into 596 million tons of helium each second; the 4 million ton mass deficit becomes 3.8 × 10²⁶ W of radiant power via E = mc²
Van de Graaff generators accumulate charge on a conducting sphere, creating potentials exceeding 100,000 V—used in nuclear physics and dramatic classroom demonstrations
Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse) travel through Earth at different speeds, allowing scientists to locate earthquake epicenters and map Earth's interior structure
Endoscopes use fiber optic bundles and tiny lens systems to allow doctors to view inside the body without surgery—used in colonoscopy, arthroscopy, and laparoscopy

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Optical Instruments.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Optical Instruments?

options.text: Scanning tunneling microscopes use quantum tunneling current between a sharp tip and a surface to image individual atoms on surfaces with sub-atomic resolution
Medical X-rays: high-energy photons penetrate soft tissue but are absorbed by dense bone, creating shadow images for diagnosing fractures and other conditions
Electron microscopes achieve resolutions of ~0.1 nm by using electron 'lenses' (magnetic fields) instead of optical lenses—visualizing individual atoms
Gas identification: each gas produces unique spectral lines—used to identify unknown gases in chemistry labs, environmental monitoring, and forensics

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Optical Instruments.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Optical Instruments in practice?

options.text: The electrical grid: power plants generate at ~20 kV, transformers step up to 110-765 kV for long-distance transmission, then step down to 120/240V for homes—reducing losses by factors of thousands
Radio reception: AM radio waves (λ ≈ 200-600m) diffract around buildings and mountains, while FM (λ ≈ 3m) requires more line-of-sight; why AM reaches farther in hilly terrain
Wireless charging (Qi standard): a changing magnetic field in the charging pad induces an EMF in the receiving coil inside the phone
The James Webb Space Telescope uses a 6.5m segmented gold-coated mirror to collect infrared light from the earliest galaxies in the universe

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Optical Instruments.
